FULL CONTENT:
# [Living Tongues Institute for Endangered Languages](https://livingtongues.org/)

# Endangered Languages Resource Page

People often approach us seeking information about the world’s endangered languages. In particular, they ask about ongoing documentation projects, regional archives, and how they can get involved.

In an effort to celebrate and promote all the diverse initiatives currently going on in the field of endangered languages, we put together this extensive resource page with links to many excellent websites.

We encourage people to explore the resources below, and also to consult our [Educational Resources](https://livingtongues.org/educational-resources/) page for links related to curriculum planning and language learning.

For further information about the field of endangered languages and our work as an institute, see also our [Glossary](https://livingtongues.org/glossary/ "Glossary") and our [FAQ](https://livingtongues.org/FAQ/). *Thank you to our wonderful volunteers in 2020 who contributed to this page!*

[![screen-shot-2012-11-20-at-3-00-07-pm](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/05/screen-shot-2012-11-20-at-3-00-07-pm.png?resize=474%2C231&ssl=1)](https://travel.nationalgeographic.com/travel/enduring-voices/)**Please note:** The regional archives listed below are grouped according to [Language Hotspots](https://livingtongues.org/language-hotspots/). We define hotspots as concentrated regions of the world having the highest level of linguistic diversity, the highest levels of endangerment, and the least-studied languages. The Language Hotspots therefore represent high-priority regions that urgently need further documentation and support.

![screen-shot-2012-11-20-at-3-00-07-pm](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/05/screen-shot-2012-11-20-at-3-00-07-pm.png?resize=474%2C231&ssl=1)

***This website has not been updated since 2020, so some links will be defunct, and others have been superseded by other initiatives and websites.***

[![archive-bg](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/archive-bg.jpg?resize=474%2C267&ssl=1)](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/archive-bg.jpg?ssl=1)

![archive-bg](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/archive-bg.jpg?resize=474%2C267&ssl=1)

**REGIONAL LANGUAGE ARCHIVES**

AFRICAN HOTSPOTS

[![email_archiving2](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/email_archiving2.png?resize=308%2C293&ssl=1)](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/email_archiving2.png?ssl=1)

![email_archiving2](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/email_archiving2.png?resize=308%2C293&ssl=1)

**GLOBAL LANGUAGE RESOURCES, ARCHIVES & MAPS**

[Endangered Languages Project:](http://www.endangeredlanguages.com) a project by the Alliance for Linguistic Diversity, this world-wide site seeks to compile up-to-date information on threatened languages. It is part of ELCat (the Endangered Language Catalogue), an online collaborative effort to protect global linguistic diversity. We are a proud partner of this initiative.

[Ethnologue](http://www.ethnologue.com/language_index.asp): This is a database for endangered language documentation maintained by the Summer Institute for Linguistics (also known as the “SIL”).

[OLAC](http://search.language-archives.org/index.html): The “Open Language Archives Community” (OLAC) is a multi institutional database of language resources that can be searched to find all kinds of data and links to archives.

[UNESCO](http://www.unesco.org/languages-atlas/): The United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) maintains the Atlas of the World’s Languages in Danger.

[WALS](https://en.wal.unesco.org/discover/languages): The “World Atlas of Language Structures” (WALS) is a database of structural information about languages, maintained by the Leipzig-based Max Plank Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology.

[Rosetta Project](http://rosettaproject.org/): This is a collaborative digital library of language research. (Note: use “advanced search” to search by all media types).

[DOBES](http://www.mpi.nl/DOBES/projects/): A project of the Volkswagen Foundation, the “Dokumentation Bedrohter Sprachen” [Documentation of Endangered Languages Programme] is a great online archive. You can search by Projects / Language Areas.

[ERIC](http://www.eric.ed.gov) Database: The Education Resources Information Center is an online digital library of education research and information.

[DELAMAN](http://www.delaman.org/ "DELAMAN"): Digital Endangered Languages and Musics Archive Network

[ELAR](http://www.hrelp.org/archive/ "ELAR"): Endangered Languages Archive

[GLOTTOLOG / LANGDOC](http://www.glottolog.org/): This vast site provides comprehensive bibliographical and other reference information for the world’s languages, especially the lesser known languages.

[MULTITREE](http://multitree.linguistlist.org/search): A Digital Library of Language Relationships

[LANGSCAPE:](http://langscape.umd.edu/) an interactive online map that allows users to discover the languages spoken at any point on the globe.

[UCLA Phonetics Archive](http://archive.phonetics.ucla.edu/): The materials on this site comprise audio recordings illustrating phonetic structures from over 200 languages with phonetic transcriptions, plus scans of original field notes where relevant.

[OPEN SOURCE TOOLS FOR ENDANGERED LANGUAGES](https://github.com/RichardLitt/endangered-languages): Compiled by Richard Littauer, this GitHub repository includes useful code and computational resources for endangered languages.

[![archives](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/archives.jpg?resize=474%2C127&ssl=1)](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/archives.jpg?ssl=1)

![archives](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2012/06/archives.jpg?resize=474%2C127&ssl=1)

**ENDANGERED LANGUAGE NUMERAL SYSTEMS**

**LANGUAGE DOCUMENTATION & CITIZEN MEDIA RESOURCES\***

**CONTACT**

# Who We Are

Living Tongues Institute for Endangered Languages (EIN: 41-2183857) is a registered 501(c)(3) organization that was founded in 2005 by linguist Gregory D. S. Anderson, and has research teams around the globe. As leaders in the field of endangered language documentation, we stand at the intersection of linguistics and community activism. 

# Visit Our Dictionary Platform

[![Living Dictionaries banner](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/LIving-Dictionaries-banner.png?fit=300%2C74&ssl=1)](https://livingdictionaries.app/)

![Living Dictionaries banner](https://i0.wp.com/livingtongues.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/LIving-Dictionaries-banner.png?fit=300%2C74&ssl=1)

Our platform is a free, mobile-friendly, multimedia dictionary-builder for under-represented languages. We serve 400+ languages worldwide, with a user interface available in 14 languages. As of 2025, our platform houses over 400k total dictionary entries.

[Start exploring now](https://livingdictionaries.app/)

--- SOURCE 3: LibGuides: Endangered languages: Research databases ---
URL: https://guides.lib.uconn.edu/c.php?g=1232158&p=9017255

SUMMARY:
These databases are primary databases for research in endangered languages, but to be exhaustive the secondary resources must also be used.

FULL CONTENT:
# Endangered languages — Research databases

## Resources for Endangered Language Research

These databases are primary databases for research in endangered languages, but to be exhaustive the secondary resources must also be used.  In all databases, it is **imperative** to use the thesaurus, for even such common terms as *sleeping language* and *second language acquisition*are indexed in different ways.

Indexes worldwide materials from the late 1800s to today in the fields of social, cultural, physical, biological, and linguistic anthropology; ethnology, archaeology, folklore, and material culture.

Indexes scholarly journals and magazines in all areas of education, from early intervention (birth to age three), preschool, K-12 (public and private), and higher education. Includes bilingual, special, gifted, and computer education.

Scholarly articles, ebooks, essay collections, working papers, proceedings, dissertations, and bibliographies in literature and linguistics.

Provides statistics on media, the environment, business and industry, sports, communication, politics, health care and more. Geographic coverage is worldwide, with particular emphasis on the United States, China, and European Union countries.

These databases often contain material that supplements the content accessible through the Linguistics-Specific Resources.  It is imperative to use the database's thesaurus when doing research. 

![Access provided by the Connecticut State Library](//d2jv02qf7xgjwx.cloudfront.net/sites/768/icons/34152/CSLIcon.png "Access provided by the Connecticut State Library  ")

Includes articles from scholarly journals and popular magazines covering a wide variety of disciplines with the humanities, social sciences, and sciences.

Article abstracts and conference documents from the world's life sciences literature, covering more than 5,200 journals.

Access to thousands of ebooks, case studies, tutorials, and videos in computer science, business, communication, and design.  *Select "not listed" when prompted to select institution and then enter UConn email address.*

Scholarly articles in psychology, plus medicine, psychiatry, education, social work, criminology, social science, business, & organizational behavior.

Covers world religions, major denominations, biblical studies, religious history, epistemology, political philosophy, philosophy of language, moral philosophy and the history of philosophy.

Scholarly journal articles about sociology and other social and behavioral sciences. Includes culture & social structure; economic development; evaluation research; family & social welfare; management and complex organizations; policy planning.

Scholarly articles, books, and reports from 1864 to present on zoology, ecology, taxonomy and biology of living and fossil animals.

[![Creative Commons](https://s3.amazonaws.com/libapps/customers/757/images/by-nc.png)](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/)
 This work is licensed under a [Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International License](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/).

--- SOURCE 4: | Endangered Languages Archive ---
URL: https://eldp.access.preservica.com/

SUMMARY:
The Endangered Languages Archive (ELAR) is a digital repository for preserving multimedia collections of endangered languages from all over the world.

FULL CONTENT:
![Endangered Languages Archive](https://eldp.access.preservica.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2020/05/logovorschlag5_left_transBG.png)![](https://eldp.access.preservica.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2019/09/P1070121_short.jpg)

# The Endangered Languages Archive

The Endangered Languages Archive (ELAR) is a digital repository for preserving multimedia collections of endangered languages from all over the world, making them available for future generations.

ELAR’s mission is to:

In ELAR’s collections you can find recordings of every-day conversations, instructions on how to build fish traps or boats, explanations of kinship systems and the use of medicinal plants, and learn about art forms like string figures and sand drawings. ELAR’s collections are unique records of local knowledge systems encoded in their languages, described by the holders of the knowledge themselves.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Resources like Ethnologue and OLAC provide extensive data on endangered languages, facilitating access to language documentation and supporting researchers in their efforts.
Local archives preserve the linguistic heritage of communities, housing multimedia collections that document cultural practices, oral traditions, and everyday conversations in endangered languages.

Scholarly articles and research papers contribute to understanding language endangerment, documenting strategies for preservation, and offering insights into linguistic diversity and community engagement.

Official documents from governmental agencies often outline policies and initiatives aimed at protecting endangered languages, providing valuable data and frameworks for language preservation efforts.

Non-governmental organizations play a crucial role in supporting endangered language documentation, offering funding, expertise, and collaborative opportunities for grassroots language revitalization projects.
